posts.text:
Is there a term for being envious of other countries expanding and modernizing their passenger rail infrastructure??

Because whatever it is, I have it as an American who lives just a few blocks away from an old passenger rail station that is now a burger restaurant..

Don’t want more data centers?

Join me in demanding that Congress pass a national moratorium on data centers at the link in the replies.

Our communities and our planet deserve better.

If you don’t want to be a paid subscriber to Good Climate News anymore, that’s fine. 

Please cancel your subscription on Substack instead of disputing the charge with your bank because I have to pay back all the money you’ve ever spent on that subscription if you do that!

How I look while watching the world finally realize that relying on fossil fuels, especially during times of conflict, is actually bad for the economy because it means the grip the fossil fuel industry has on the world is starting to unravel right before my very eyes:

Just a heads up that young people won't engage with your scientific research or activism campaigns if you use generative AI images and videos.

They probably won't trust you or your organization moving forward ether, so hire a creative like the good ol' days or don't use any graphics at all.
Need a bit of good news to end the work week? I cover economic growth spurred by climate solutions in China, legacy PFAS levels declining in North Atlantic Pilot whales, and more!

The additional cost of ONE fossil fuel price spike on the scale of 2022 = the ENTIRE COST of Net Zero by 2050. We get precisely nothing in return for the first cost, and a whole new, more secure and cheaper energy system from the second one. 
#NoBrainer
www.theccc.org.uk/2026/03/11/c...

It's been interesting to watch major media outlets put their climate reporting behind a paywall while other articles are not.

I understand the financial reasoning but worry about its overall impact. Climate hushing is bad enough as is; this is only going to make it worse.

It’s almost like collaborating peacefully to build clean energy projects (and not fighting wars over fossil fuels) is the future.

Read more: goodclimatenews.substack.com/p/edition-38...

Reverend Jesse Jackson called on each of us to be heralds of change, to be messengers of hope; to step forward and say “Send me” wherever we have a chance to make an impact.

How fortunate we were that Jesse Jackson answered that call. What a great debt we owe to him.

Beware of bad actor messaging campaigns aimed to get you to not take action. They are popping up on my feed here more often.

One just said that “voting truly is the opiate of the masses,” as if voting isn’t the bare minimum way we should engage in our democracy.

Please keep voting 🙏
Yeah, but for a climate journalist to write an op-ed without talking to activists and then claim we aren’t doing anything is just straight up unprofessional.. even in times of fascism..
The case for #cleanenergy gets better by the day.

Has anyone had luck with using bentonite clay as a plastic-free pond liner?

I've used it to patch holes in landfill liners for years, and I can't believe I didn't consider it sooner for my backyard wildlife pond design.

This is a marvelous change not only for the Humpback whale 🐋 population but for all of us. We need to keep our gentle giants from going extinct.
Fifty years after New Zealand stopped whaling, humpback population showing signs of recovery | RNZ News share.google/X1HB3JFgaePK...
I love how I've trained my Bluesky algorithim to only show me climate, politics, trains, mushrooms, birds, and Star Trek. It's truly a breath of fresh air compared to the rest of social media.
Wildlife crossings are quickly becoming popular in the United States because they protect people, animals, and the economy all at once.
Well, this was insulting to read as a scientist and activist who has been working overtime for the past year to reduce the damage the Trump administration is doing.

Edition 38: March 11, 2026. The Trump administration might be doing everything they can to increase fossil fuel production, but the rest of the world is still committed to expanding clean energy capacity.
Edition 37: February 20, 2026. From banks refusing to financial a controversial fossil fuel project to groups suing the EPA over revoking the endangerment finding, our climate activisim is working!
Edition 36: February 13, 2026. From doubling tiger populations to Africa showing signs of a solar boom, there’s a lot to be hopeful about despite all the bad in the world.

Edition 35: February 6, 2026. Between Europe hitting a positive climate tipping point and the Trump administration failing yet again to stop offshore wind, it’s nice to be reminded that not all hope is lost.
Edition 34: January 23, 2026. Coal Generation Declines in China and India for The First Time in Over 50 Years
Edition 33: December 19, 2025. It's the second half of my favorite stories of progress on climate from 2025 because we could all use a reminder that this year didn't completely suck.

Edition 32: December 12, 2025. 2025 is almost over, so that means it's time to share my favorite stories of progress on climate from the past year. Part II coming next week because there were too many stories for just one edition!
Edition 31: December 5, 2025. From floods to fires to big polluters taking over negotiations, COP30 felt like a climate disaster fever dream, but that doesn't mean there wasn't any progress.
No New Edition This Week!. I caught a bad cold over the weekend, so stayed tuned next week for a special edition focused on the good and bad that came out of COP30 in Brazil.

Edition 30: November 15, 2025. From a global treaty on overfishing going into effect to Colombia buying solar panels for low income households, there is still plenty of hope when it comes to the climate crisis.
Edition 29: November 7, 2025. The time for big polluters to be actually held accountable is so close I can taste it!
Edition 28: October 31, 2025. Happy Halloween! Please enjoy this special edition looking at progress on conservation from the IUCN World Conservation Congress.
Edition 27: October 21, 2025. Community solar has officially arrived in Scotland, Danish chemists have invented a way to turn plastic waste into a carbon capture material, and more!

Stay Tuned for More Good News! . This past week I’ve been a bit busy virtually attending the IUCN World Conservation Congress and addressing some very valid fears about the Climate Clock activism art piece and the recent paper suggesting the world is about to hit its first climate tipping point
Edition 26: October 10, 2025. From hitting historic clean energy milestones to protecting climate-resilient coral reefs, there is still plenty of good climate news out there.
It’s almost like collaborating peacefully to build clean energy projects (and not fighting wars over fossil fuels) is the future.
The case for #cleanenergy gets better by the day.
Wildlife crossings are quickly becoming  popular in the United States because they protect people, animals, and the economy all at once 👏

When your home is over 100 years old, you can’t just clean it like every other house. Original woodwork, vintage tile, aged glass. One wrong product and you could be damaging something irreplaceable. That’s why I shop at @Grove Collaborative. Plant-based, cruelty-free, and formulated without the harsh synthetic chemicals that wreck sensitive surfaces (and the ecosystems they get washed into). Link in bio to shop my Grove favorites — your home (and the planet) will thank you. #GrovePartner #Histo
I’ve got more calls to action about stopping data centers coming very soon, but for now, let’s focus on finding ways to eat less or no meat and dairy.
I heard y’all needed some #goodnews 🫡
There’s still good happening in the world, and here’s proof 🫶
Research like Dr. Susie Dai’s is what gives me hope that we’ll solve the plastic waste crisis 👏
Reposting this because the #climateclock is doing its rounds on social media again and causing a lot of panic in the process.
A lot of times climate solutions are just modernizing the way we used to do things, and this wind-powered cargo ship is no exception!
